item.title: A woman in Auschwitz
item.description:
Gisella Perl was born and grew up in Sighetu in Romania. She was the first Jewish graduate from the University Medical School in Kolosvar, during which time she did some of her study in Berlin. She practiced as a gynecologist in Sighetu with her husband Dr Krauss, and had a son & daughter. In 1944, as the previously anti-Semitic but nominally independent Romanian government began to seek a way out of the Axis Pact and make peace with the Allies before the country was defeated by the USSR, the Nazis took direct control over many areas of Romania, including Sighetu, and enacted their "Final Solution" policy of deporting Jews to concentration camps for forced labour and extermination. Dr Perl's daughter went missing during the process of deportation. Upon arrival at Auschwitz, Dr Perl - inmate number 25,404 - was identified as a gynaecologist and brought to the Nazi Dr Josef Mengele (1911-1979), who was engaged in a series of human "experiments" of extreme brutality. This included the vivisection of pregnant women at various stages of pregnancy. Dr Perl was set the task of 'caring' for those who were set aside for Dr Mengele's experiments, with minimal equipment and resources.
[Picture: Dr Perl as a medical student.] The vast majority of Jewish women who arrived at the concentration camps detectably pregnant were gassed on arrival as unsuitable for forced labour, while those who managed to conceal their pregnancies were ultimately faced with a choice to either attempt an abortion, or allow the child to be born (which would require the risky co-operation of other prisoners to conceal) and then kill the baby; babies who were 'captured' by the Nazis were in some cases killed with lethal injections, but more commonly were drowned in buckets. Dr Perl was instructed directly by Dr Mengele that all pregnant prisoners were to be reported to him so he could select experimentation victims. At this point, she resolved that "there would be no pregnant women in Auschwitz". Dr Perl, who had strong professional and religious beliefs about abortion, made the difficult decision that to save women's lives, she must perform abortions. Without any medical instruments or anaesthesia, and often in the cramped and filthy bunks within the women’s barracks of Auschwitz, Perl ended the lives of countless foetuses, in hope that the mothers would survive and later, perhaps, be able to bear children. In some instances, the pregnancy was too far along to be able to perform an abortion. Perl would pierce the amniotic sac and manually dilate the mother’s cervix with her bare hands to induce labour. In these cases, the premature infants died, almost instantly. With the threat of a discovered pregnancy removed, the women were able to continue to work, gaining what for many was still only a temporary reprieve from their death sentences.
[Picture: Dr Josef Mengele, he escaped the collapse of Nazi Germany and died in 1979 after suffering a stroke while swimming in the sea off Bertioga, Sao Paulo, Brazil. His body was disinterred in 1985 and positively identified. He did not initially live "in hiding" in South America, he even requested - and received - a copy of his birth certificate from West Germany in 1956.] Amongst women prisoners, amenorrhoea (the ceasing of menstruation due to stress or physical deprivation) was very common, often causing additional mental horror to women who wondered that, even if they lived, they may never be able to have children. Amenorrhoea was not universal however; women were not provided with any kind of menstrual sanitary products, leading to a gendered black market of bundled rags which were jealously guarded. Menstruation not being a controllable biological process, many women suffered events like blood-dripping down their legs while being 'on parade' for roll call outside their barracks. Many survivors noted that continuing menstruation had the "advantage" of reducing sexual assaults from Guards, while Elisabeth Feldman De Jong (1916-2009), who suffered amenorrhoea, discovered that menstruating would dissuade Nazi doctors like Mengele from undergoing 'experiments' on women's reproductive organs, so borrowed the bloodied underwear of her menstruating sister to wear during inspections by doctors.
[Picture: Women assigned to slave labour, Auschwitz, at some point in 1944.] Amidst the horror, one notable event of solidarity repeated in many sources is of women consoling and teaching teenagers who had their first periods in the camps, whose own mothers were already dead and so unable to teach them. By day, Perl assisted Mengele in his "research". He commanded her to deliver the first pair of twins born in Auschwitz, which she knew were destined for his infamous twin experiments. At one point he ordered the extraction of an eight-week-old foetus from a pregnant woman – intact – and preserved in a glass jar so it could be sent to Berlin. Mengele was deeply committed to the nonsense Nazi concept of "annihilating the biological basis of Jewry". Dr Perl was transferred to Bergen-Belsen camp as the Nazi situation on the Eastern Front fell apart, and was at this camp on April 15th 1945 when she was liberated by British forces amidst a raging typhus epidemic. On the same day, she helped deliver a healthy, free, baby.
[Picture: Dr Perl's book, which reads very much like an exorcism.] It is unclear how many babies were aborted or born & killed in the Nazi concentration camps; it is however notable that at liberation, other ethnicities and political prisoners were found to have some hundreds of babies amidst those liberated, while amongst Jews babies numbered in the dozens, which suggests that the Nazis took a special interest in the murder of their primary bogeyman's children. Dr Perl was initially arrested by the Allies as a co-conspirator in Nazi crimes against humanity, but upon being established as an unwilling victim, she provided testimony at various trials of Nazis. She eventually established that her son and husband had both perished in the camps, along with her parents and a greater part of her extended family. She attempted to kill herself and was hospitalised in a French convent-hospital until 1947. She was then joyfully re-united with her daughter, Gabriella, who had been taken in by a non-Jewish family in 1944, and so survived the war.
[Picture: Perl, bottom left, the only female doctor on staff at the Department of Obstetrics and Gynecology at Mount Sinai Hospital in autumn 1966.] Dr Perl wrote a book in 1948 about her experiences in Auschwitz, which led to her meeting Eleanor Roosevelt (1884-1962), and it was with her support that Dr Perl became a US citizen in 1951 and opened a private obstetric practice in Manhattan. The majority of her patients were Holocaust survivors. She eventually became an infertility specialist at Mount Sinai Hospital, Manhattan; where she worked habitually with her sleeves rolled up and Auschwitz number on display. She would go on to deliver more than 3,000 babies. Dr Perl died in 1988 in Herzliya, Israel, where she lived with her daughter.

item.title: Know your carabiner
item.description: In 1910 Otto "Rambo" Herzog (1888-1964) a German climber and inventor repurposed for climbing a belt clasp he saw worn by a fire brigade and that's how we got the carabiner we know today
item.dc:creator: kbowser

item.title: Day 258: of cheeseburgers I want inside of me

item.title: US Concrete Workers have been on strike since November. Not a whisper in news.
item.description:
Started with 30 workers and is now hundreds strong between Seattle and Portland.
Stand with the working class.
They should be able to afford to live in the cities they build. https://apnews.com/article/business-seattle-labor-negotiations-strikes-8b07940a4b7831123f2c6491e3754da9 Anna on TYT informed me about this today https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=BNqtRnJuMPg
item.dc:creator: iusedtodream

item.title: Shartbucks
item.dc:creator: mfrybeasley

item.title: Idk who needs to hear it, but this is what suicidal depression can look like
item.description: Me, 5 years ago, pregnant with the Man Cub. My whole life, I looked forward to being a mom more than anything else. I was beyond ecstatic when the stick turned blue! But then the morning sickness turned into hyperemesis gravidarum. The (now ex) husband turned into the adulterer. The happiest time in my life turned into multiple hospital stays for physical and mental health reasons. I went from wanting nothing more than picking names and decorating a nursery to afraid to drive because I kept having very, very vivid fantasies of crashing my car into a guard rail. Parenthood is HARD. Pregnancy is HARD. Asking for help during what’s supposed to be the most magical, happy time in your life is HARD. For anyone who needs a soft spot to land when life is hard, I’m here. FP Edit. To everyone who has shared kind words -both to me & other commenters- and/or been open about their own struggles-thank you!!! The whole reason I shared this story was because I’d gone to a PPA/PPD support group at 4 weeks postpartum…and was the only. fucking. person who showed up. So not only did I feel like a horrible horrendous failure of a mom but I felt like I was the ONLY horrible horrendous failure of a mom. No one should ever feel that alone. I’m so grateful for everyone who has contributed to helping other women know how normal this is and helping them find courage to ask for help.
item.dc:creator: H0llyheadHarpy

item.title: Wow! Incredibly powerful!
item.description: https://twitter.com/garychambersjr/status/1491397288790740994?s=21 “‘Scars and Bars.’ Here in Louisiana and all around the South, it feels like Jim Crow never left and the remnants of the Confederacy remain. I do believe the South will rise again, but this time, it’ll be on our terms.” Register to vote and/or check the status of your voter registration by going here: IWillVote.com Be sure to vote in every election, including the “small” ones. The “big” politicians often got their start by winning the small elections. You can help weed out the bad ones early. #vote
item.dc:creator: SisterMidnight

item.title: First tattoo and it only took 2, 5 hour sessions...
item.description: Edit: Thank you all for the kind words! I'm glad a lot of people like the style and details! I've had a lot of comments asking for my artist. He is based out of Grand Blanc. Michigan. Rad dude. You can find him on Instagram at @jessevickerstattoos He is typically booked out a year in advance, and his pieces costs a bit, but he does amazing work and it's worth it!
item.dc:creator: dinosaurgummy
